Category: --- PRE-CONSTRUCTION: design, estimating, permitting, and planning before ground-breaking ---

Description: Owner defines project requirements and validates viability — needs assessment, site analysis, zoning review, pro forma development, and environmental studies. Gate: program approval / authorization to proceed with design. Primarily owner and development team; contractor involved only in CM-at-Risk or design-build.
Industry Standard: ASTM E1557 (UNIFORMAT II); local zoning codes; NEPA/CEQA (environmental)
Common Values: Pro forma; feasibility study; site due diligence report; environmental assessment; program document
Source Systems: Excel; Argus (real estate pro forma); GIS tools; Bluebeam; local permit portals
Procore Coverage: None — Procore is a construction-phase platform with no feasibility or programming module
Benchmarking Use: Compare go/no-go decision timelines and site assessment scope across project types
Notes: Cross-domain: Project Attributes (project type, location, total value defined here). Duration ranges from weeks to years depending on entitlement complexity.

Description: Architects and engineers develop the design from concept through construction documents — schematic design (SD), design development (DD), construction documents (CD), code review, structural/MEP engineering, energy modeling, specifications. Gate: bid-ready construction documents issued.
Industry Standard: AIA B101 (design services); AIA E203 (BIM); IBC/ICC (code compliance); ASHRAE 90.1 (energy)
Common Values: SD package; DD drawings; construction documents; specifications; code compliance report; design-phase RFIs
Source Systems: AutoCAD; Revit; Bluebeam; Newforma; ProjectWise; BIM 360 Design; Procore (drawings)
Procore Coverage: Low — drawing management and design-phase RFIs exist, but Procore is not a design tool
Benchmarking Use: Compare design duration and RFI volume across delivery methods (design-build vs. design-bid-build)
Notes: Design RFIs between owner/architect/engineers are high volume. Coordination between disciplines starts here. Cross-domain: Building Elements (all systems defined on paper).

Description: Develop cost estimates from conceptual through detailed GMP or hard bid — quantity takeoff, unit pricing, subcontractor pricing, risk contingency, value engineering. Multiple estimate rounds: conceptual (SD), detailed (DD), final (CD). Gate: budget or GMP established.
Industry Standard: AACE 18R-97 (cost estimate classification); ASTM E1557 (UNIFORMAT II); RSMeans
Common Values: Conceptual estimate; detailed estimate; GMP proposal; value engineering log; bid tabulation
Source Systems: On-Screen Takeoff; Planswift; HCSS HeavyBid; Sage Estimating; ProEst; Excel; Procore Estimating
Procore Coverage: Medium — precon estimating tables exist (estimates, estimate_layers) but adoption is growing
Benchmarking Use: Compare estimate accuracy (final vs. actual) and VE savings rates across project types
Notes: VE can significantly change scope. Cross-domain: Financial (budget line items and cost codes originate from estimates).

Description: Secure regulatory approvals required before construction — building permits, zoning variances, environmental permits, utility coordination, fire marshal review, DOT permits, stormwater permits. Gate: building permit issued / notice to proceed.
Industry Standard: IBC/ICC (building code); NFPA (fire code); EPA/state environmental; ADA; local zoning ordinances
Common Values: Building permit; environmental permits; utility agreements; fire marshal approval; SWPPP
Source Systems: Local permit portals; e-permitting systems; Bluebeam; Excel; Procore (document management)
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated permitting module; documents stored in project files
Benchmarking Use: Compare permit timelines across jurisdictions and project complexity levels
Notes: Permit timelines vary wildly — 2 weeks to 12+ months depending on jurisdiction. Critical path item. Cross-domain: Project Attributes (location drives requirements).

Description: GC develops execution strategy before breaking ground — baseline schedule, logistics plan, phasing strategy, safety plan, quality plan, procurement strategy, staffing plan. CM-at-Risk does this during design; design-bid-build GCs do it after award. Gate: baseline schedule accepted.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (scheduling); AGC (safety planning); OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (safety); CPM scheduling standards
Common Values: Baseline schedule; site logistics plan; phasing plan; safety plan; quality plan; procurement schedule
Source Systems: Oracle P6; MS Project; Asta Powerproject; Procore (schedule); Bluebeam; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — schedule module exists with schedule_task and lookahead tables, but baseline tracking is limited
Benchmarking Use: Compare planning duration and baseline schedule quality across delivery methods
Notes: Baseline schedule is a key contractual deliverable. Cross-domain: Resources (staffing plan), Project Attributes (delivery method determines timing).

Category: --- PROCUREMENT: bidding, buyout, submittals, fabrication, and material delivery ---

Description: Solicit bids from subcontractors and suppliers, level bids, negotiate scope, and award contracts — scope definition, bid solicitation, bid leveling, insurance/bonding verification, contract execution. Buyout typically 60-80% complete before mobilization. Gate: all major subcontracts and POs executed.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (general conditions); ConsensusDocs; AGC contract forms; state lien law
Common Values: Subcontracts; purchase orders; bid tabulations; scope clarification letters; insurance certificates; bonds
Source Systems: Building Connected; PlanHub; SmartBid; iSqFt; Procore (bidding); Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — bidding module exists in Procore but bid tables not in analytics gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Compare buyout timelines and bid coverage rates across project sizes
Notes: Long-lead items (steel, curtain wall, elevators, switchgear) bid early. Cross-domain: Financial (subcontracts become commitments).

Description: Subcontractors prepare detailed fabrication and product documents for architect/engineer review — submittal preparation, review cycles, resubmittals, product substitution requests. Submittal log is a critical schedule driver. Gate: all critical-path submittals approved.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat (section-based organization); AIA A201 (submittal requirements); ASTM product standards
Common Values: Approved submittals; approved shop drawings; product data sheets; material samples; color selections
Source Systems: Procore (submittals); Submittal Exchange; PlanGrid; BIM 360; Bluebeam; Newforma
Procore Coverage: High — submittals table with status, due dates, overdue flags, and approval tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare submittal cycle times and resubmittal rates across project types and trades
Notes: Curtain wall, structural steel, and elevator submittals typically have longest lead. Cross-domain: Building Elements (submittals organized by system).

Description: Materials fabricated off-site with delivery schedules established — structural steel (8-16 wks), curtain wall (12-24 wks), elevators (16-30 wks), switchgear (12-20 wks), precast, HVAC equipment, custom casework. Gate: long-lead materials delivered or delivery confirmed.
Industry Standard: AISC (steel fabrication); AAMA (curtain wall); ASME (elevators); UL (electrical equipment)
Common Values: Fabricated materials; delivery schedules; factory test reports; QC documentation
Source Systems: ERP systems; fabricator tracking portals; Excel; Procore (submittals as proxy for fabrication status)
Procore Coverage: Low — no fabrication tracking module; submittals used as proxy for fabrication status
Benchmarking Use: Compare fabrication lead times by material type across market conditions
Notes: These durations drive the critical path schedule. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Structural Frame, Exterior Enclosure, Elevators, Electrical).

Description: Standard materials ordered and delivery sequenced to match construction schedule — PO issuance, delivery scheduling, staging area planning, just-in-time coordination, material tracking. Material shortages and delivery delays are a top cause of schedule disruption. Gate: materials staged for installation start.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat (material sections); ASTM (material standards)
Common Values: Purchase orders; delivery schedules; material tracking logs; receiving reports; shortage notifications
Source Systems: Procore (daily log deliveries); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Excel; supplier portals
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_delivery tracks on-site deliveries; daily_log_delay has structured Material delay type (84K records)
Benchmarking Use: Compare material delay frequency and duration across project types and geographies
Notes: Staging space is a constant constraint on urban sites. Cross-domain: Resources (materials), Material Shortage (insight #2 — delay_type = Material is primary signal).

Category: --- CONSTRUCTION – EARLY: mobilization, sitework, foundations, and underground work ---

Description: Establish temporary facilities and infrastructure to support construction — temporary fencing, construction entrance, trailer placement, temporary utilities (power, water, sanitation), site signage, erosion control, tree protection. First impression for owner and community. Gate: site ready for production work. Duration: 1-3%.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (site safety); EPA NPDES (stormwater — SWPPP); local ordinances
Common Values: Site logistics plan implemented; temporary utilities active; SWPPP installed; construction entrance operational
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs); Raken; Fieldwire; PlanGrid; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs track site activity but no dedicated mobilization workflow
Benchmarking Use: Compare mobilization duration across project sizes and site constraint levels
Notes: SWPPP must be in place before disturbing soil. Temporary power is critical path for MEP startup. Cross-domain: Project Attributes (site logistics driven by location).

Description: Establish safety culture and environmental compliance from day one — site-specific safety orientation, toolbox talks, emergency action plan, hospital route, environmental awareness training, hazard communication. Every worker must complete orientation. Gate: all workers oriented and safety systems active.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926.21 (safety training); OSHA 1926.35 (emergency action plans); EPA environmental awareness
Common Values: Safety orientation records; emergency action plan; site-specific safety plan; environmental compliance binder
Source Systems: Procore (observations, safety logs); Safety Reports; iAuditor; SiteDocs; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — safety observations and daily_log_safety_violation tables exist; orientation tracking is partial
Benchmarking Use: Compare orientation completion rates and initial safety incident rates across projects
Notes: Sets the safety culture for the entire project. Cross-domain: Recurring Violations (insight #11 — safety observation patterns start here).

Description: Remove existing structures and vegetation to prepare for new construction — building demolition, selective demolition, clearing and grubbing, underground obstruction removal, hazmat abatement if required. Gate: site cleared and ready for earthwork. Duration: 2-5%.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926.850 (demolition safety); EPA NESHAP (hazmat/asbestos); local demolition permits
Common Values: Cleared site; hazmat clearance reports; demolition completion certificate; waste manifests
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs); Bluebeam; environmental consultants; waste hauling systems; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and observations track demolition activity; no dedicated demolition workflow
Benchmarking Use: Compare demolition duration and hazmat abatement frequency across project types
Notes: Hazmat abatement (asbestos, lead, contaminated soil) can be critical path. Unknown conditions are a common claims source.

Description: Excavate and prepare the ground for foundations — bulk excavation, rock removal, shoring/sheeting, dewatering, proof rolling, structural fill, compaction, fine grading. Differing site conditions are the #1 source of construction claims. Gate: subgrade approved for foundation work. Duration: 5-10%.
Industry Standard: ASTM D698/D1557 (compaction testing); OSHA 29 CFR 1926.650 (excavation safety); geotechnical standards
Common Values: Excavation complete; compaction test reports; dewatering system operational; shoring installed
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, RFIs); Trimble (GPS grading); Topcon; survey equipment; geotechnical labs
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs, RFIs (differing conditions), and observations track earthwork; no quantity tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare earthwork duration and differing-conditions RFI rates across geographies and soil types
Notes: Dewatering can run for months on high water table sites. Compaction testing is quality-critical. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Site & Earthwork).

Description: Install underground infrastructure before building foundations cover access — sanitary sewer, storm drainage, domestic and fire water, gas service, electrical and telecom ductbank, grease interceptors. Must complete before foundation/slab work. Gate: utilities tested and backfilled. Duration: 3-6%.
Industry Standard: International Plumbing Code; NFPA 24 (fire water); local utility standards; ASTM testing standards
Common Values: Utility installations tested; pressure/vacuum/mandrel test reports; as-built survey of utility locations
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs); Civil 3D; utility locating services; survey equipment
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections and daily logs track utility work; no dedicated underground utility module
Benchmarking Use: Compare utility installation duration and test failure rates across project types
Notes: Utility conflicts with foundations are common — as-built surveys critical for future reference. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Site Utilities).

Description: Construct below-grade structural support — pile driving or drilled shafts, spread footings, grade beams, foundation walls, waterproofing, backfill, underslab plumbing and electrical. Concrete testing (cylinders) is mandatory. Gate: foundations complete and ready for slab/structure. Duration: 5-10%.
Industry Standard: ACI 318 (concrete design); ASTM C31/C39 (concrete testing); IBC Chapter 18 (foundations); ASTM D1143 (piles)
Common Values: Foundation as-built survey; concrete test reports; pile driving records; waterproofing inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs); concrete testing labs; pile driving analyzers; survey equipment
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections (hold points), daily logs, and RFIs track foundation work; concrete tests not structured
Benchmarking Use: Compare foundation duration and concrete test pass rates across project types and soil conditions
Notes: Underslab MEP rough-in must complete before slab pour — coordination milestone. Waterproofing inspection is a hold point. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Foundations).

Description: Pour ground-level concrete floor slab — vapor barrier, underslab insulation, rebar/WWF placement, embedded conduit, concrete placement, finishing, curing, control joint sawing. Large pours are major production events. Gate: slab cured — building ready for vertical construction. Duration: 2-4%.
Industry Standard: ACI 302.1R (floor slab construction); ASTM E1155 (FF/FL testing); ACI 360R (slabs on ground)
Common Values: Slab on grade complete; concrete test reports; floor flatness/levelness (FF/FL) test reports; cure complete
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections); concrete testing labs; laser screed equipment; FF/FL testing equipment
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs capture pour events; inspection hold points tracked; no structured FF/FL data
Benchmarking Use: Compare slab FF/FL scores and pour productivity rates across project types
Notes: FF/FL testing required for specified tolerances. Cure time (typically 28 days full strength) affects loading schedule. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Foundations).

Category: --- CONSTRUCTION – STRUCTURE: vertical construction — structural frame and building envelope ---

Description: Erect the steel frame including columns, beams, girders, metal deck, and shear studs — connection bolting/welding, plumbing and aligning. Tower crane operations dominate logistics. Fall protection is the primary safety concern. Gate: steel topped out (last piece set). Duration: 10-15%.
Industry Standard: AISC 360 (steel design); AISC 303 (standard practice); AWS D1.1 (welding); OSHA 29 CFR 1926.750 (steel erection)
Common Values: Steel erection complete per area; connection inspection reports; bolt torque records; welding inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, observations); Tekla; SDS2; Fabtrol; steel erector tracking
Procore Coverage: High — daily logs (manpower, equipment), inspections (connections), observations (safety), RFIs all active
Benchmarking Use: Compare steel erection rates (tons/day) and connection inspection pass rates across project sizes
Notes: Connection inspections (bolts and welds) are quality-critical hold points. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Structural Frame), Resources (ironworkers, cranes).

Description: Place concrete on metal deck or formwork for elevated floor slabs — deck prep, rebar/post-tensioning placement, embedded items, concrete pumping, finishing, curing, PT stressing. Follows deck erection floor-by-floor. Gate: floor slabs complete and cured for loading.
Industry Standard: ACI 318 (concrete design); PTI DC20.9 (post-tensioning); ASTM C31/C39 (concrete testing)
Common Values: Concrete test reports; PT stressing records; floor survey; embedded item as-builts
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections); concrete testing labs; PT stressing equipment; survey tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs track pours; inspections track hold points; PT stressing data not structured
Benchmarking Use: Compare pour cycle times per floor and concrete test results across structural systems
Notes: PT stressing has specific timing requirements. Slab edge forming at curtain wall line is coordination-critical. Duration included in structural steel.

Description: For concrete-framed buildings: form and pour columns, beams, and slabs floor-by-floor — formwork erection, rebar placement, embedded items, concrete placement, form stripping, shoring/reshoring, curing. Formwork cycle time drives schedule. Gate: concrete frame topped out. Duration: 10-15%.
Industry Standard: ACI 318 (concrete design); ACI 347 (formwork); ASTM C31/C39 (testing); IBC Chapter 19
Common Values: Concrete test reports; form strip records; reshoring plan compliance; floor survey
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs); formwork systems (PERI, Doka); concrete testing labs
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and inspections track concrete work; formwork cycle data not structured
Benchmarking Use: Compare floor cycle times and concrete strength development across project types
Notes: Alternative to steel frame. Reshoring requirements affect lower floor loading and when MEP can start. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Structural Frame).

Description: Install permanent stairs, handrails, embed plates, lintels, shelf angles, bollards, and equipment supports. Shelf angles for masonry/exterior panels must be in place before enclosure starts. Stairs provide worker access between floors. Gate: all structural and misc metals complete.
Industry Standard: AISC (misc metals); AWS D1.1 (welding); ADA/ICC A117.1 (stair dimensions and handrails)
Common Values: Stairs installed; misc metals complete per area; welding inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections); Tekla; steel fabricator tracking; survey equipment
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and inspections track installation; no dedicated misc metals tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare misc metals completion timing relative to structure completion milestone
Notes: Shelf angles are coordination-critical — bridge between structure and enclosure phases. Duration included in structure. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Structural Frame, Exterior Enclosure).

Description: Install exterior glass and aluminum systems to enclose the building — curtain wall units (unitized or stick-built), windows, storefronts, structural silicone, perimeter sealant. Long-lead fabrication means installation readiness is schedule-critical. Water testing is a major quality milestone. Gate: curtain wall substantially complete. Duration: 5-10%.
Industry Standard: AAMA 501 (water testing); ASTM E283/E331 (air/water performance); NFPA 285 (fire testing); IGCC (glazing)
Common Values: Water test reports; installation progress by floor; sealant inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, observations, RFIs); curtain wall fabricator systems; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — observations (quality), inspections, RFIs (details), daily logs, coordination issues all active
Benchmarking Use: Compare curtain wall installation rates and water test pass rates across project types
Notes: MEP penetration coordination through curtain wall is essential. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Exterior Enclosure), Procurement (longest fabrication lead).

Description: Install opaque exterior wall systems — brick veneer, precast panels, metal panels, backup wall, through-wall flashing, weep systems, control joints. Weather-sensitive: masonry can't be laid below 40°F without protection. Gate: opaque walls substantially complete. Duration: 5-10%.
Industry Standard: TMS 402/602 (masonry); PCI MNL-117 (precast tolerances); ASTM C270 (mortar); SMACNA (metal panels)
Common Values: Masonry completion per area; panel erection reports; flashing inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, observations, inspections, RFIs); Bluebeam; survey equipment; precast erector systems
Procore Coverage: High — observations (quality — mortar joints, flashing), inspections, daily logs all track masonry work
Benchmarking Use: Compare masonry/cladding installation rates and flashing deficiency rates across climate zones
Notes: Flashing and weep details critical for long-term water management — hidden work with major consequences. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Exterior Enclosure).

Description: Establish continuous air and moisture barrier across the building envelope — fluid-applied or sheet membrane, transition detailing at openings, penetration sealing, inspection and testing. Hidden work — once cladding covers it, inspection is impossible. Gate: air barrier complete and inspected. Duration: 2-4%.
Industry Standard: ASTM E2178/E2357 (air barrier testing); ABAA (Air Barrier Association); IBC Section 2603; energy codes
Common Values: Air barrier inspection reports; adhesion test reports; continuity inspection sign-off
Source Systems: Procore (observations, inspections); specialty applicator QC systems; third-party testing firms; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — observations and inspections track barrier work; no dedicated envelope commissioning module
Benchmarking Use: Compare air barrier deficiency rates and third-party inspection pass rates across project types
Notes: Many jurisdictions require third-party inspection. Failures cause long-term moisture damage. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Exterior Enclosure).

Description: Install roof membrane system to achieve weather-tight condition — roof insulation, cover board, membrane installation, flashing, sheet metal, roof drainage, penetration detailing. Dry-in is a major project milestone enabling interior work. Gate: roof substantially complete — building is weather-tight. Duration: 3-5%.
Industry Standard: NRCA (roofing standards); ASTM D4434/D6878 (membrane); FM Global (insurance requirements); SMACNA (sheet metal)
Common Values: Roof completion certificate; manufacturer warranty; water test reports; flashing inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, observations, inspections); Bluebeam; manufacturer rep tracking; roofing contractor QC
Procore Coverage: High — daily logs, observations (quality), inspections all track roofing; manufacturer rep visits documented
Benchmarking Use: Compare dry-in milestone achievement rates and roofing deficiency rates across project types
Notes: Dry-in is one of the most important project milestones — enables all interior work. Manufacturer rep on-site during install is typical for warranty. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Roofing).

Category: --- CONSTRUCTION – FIT-OUT: MEP rough-in, interior framing, finishes, and device installation ---

Description: Resolve spatial conflicts between MEP systems before installation — 3D coordination / BIM clash detection, routing prioritization, trade coordination meetings, clearance verification. The difference between a smooth MEP install and chaos. Gate: coordination drawings approved. Duration: 1-2% (overlaps with installation).
Industry Standard: BIM Execution Plan (AGC/AIA); LOD Spec (BIMForum); ASHRAE (duct sizing); SMACNA (duct standards)
Common Values: Coordinated MEP drawings; clash resolution log; signed-off coordination sections
Source Systems: Procore (coordination issues); Navisworks; BIM 360 Glue/Coordinate; Revit; Trimble Connect
Procore Coverage: Medium — coordination_issue table exists with issue types (Clash, Coordination, Design Review)
Benchmarking Use: Compare clash detection rates and coordination issue resolution times across project complexity
Notes: Trades that install without coordination cause rework. BIM coordination standard on $10M+ projects. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC, Plumbing, Electrical, Fire Protection).

Description: Install ductwork and hydronic piping — main duct and branch installation, HVAC piping (chilled/hot water), equipment pads, duct and pipe insulation, hanger installation. Ductwork is the largest spatial consumer in the ceiling. Gate: HVAC rough-in complete and pressure tested. Duration: 5-8%.
Industry Standard: SMACNA (duct construction); ASHRAE (design); ASTM (insulation); IMC (mechanical code); NFPA 90A
Common Values: HVAC rough-in complete per area; pressure test reports; insulation inspection; hanger inspection
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs, observations); AutoSprink; Revit MEP; HVAC contractor systems
Procore Coverage: High — daily logs, inspections, RFIs, observations, and coordination issues all active during HVAC install
Benchmarking Use: Compare HVAC rough-in duration per floor and inspection pass rates across building types
Notes: Main duct runs installed first to establish routing priority. Insulation must be inspected before ceiling closes. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC).

Description: Install supply and waste piping — domestic water distribution, sanitary waste and vent, gas piping, pipe insulation, fire stopping at penetrations. Testing is a critical hold point: pressure test for supply, DWV test for waste. Gate: plumbing rough-in complete and tested. Duration: 4-6%.
Industry Standard: International Plumbing Code (IPC); UPC; ASTM (pipe standards); NFPA (gas piping)
Common Values: Plumbing rough-in complete per area; pressure test reports; DWV test reports; fire stop inspection
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs); Revit MEP; plumbing contractor systems; testing equipment
Procore Coverage: High — inspections (pressure and DWV tests), daily logs, RFIs, observations track plumbing work
Benchmarking Use: Compare plumbing test pass rates and fire stop deficiency rates across project types
Notes: Fire stopping at penetrations is a code requirement and major punch item generator. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Plumbing).

Description: Install conduit, wire, and power distribution — main conduit and feeder installation, branch circuits, wire pulling, panel and switchgear installation, transformer setting, cable tray, grounding. Permanent power energization is a major milestone. Gate: switchgear energized. Duration: 4-6%.
Industry Standard: NEC/NFPA 70 (electrical code); NETA (testing); IEEE (power distribution); OSHA electrical safety
Common Values: Electrical rough-in complete per area; meggering reports; panel schedule verification; switchgear energization
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs); Revit MEP; Accubid/Trimble; electrical contractor systems
Procore Coverage: High — daily logs, inspections, RFIs, observations track electrical work across all phases
Benchmarking Use: Compare switchgear energization milestone timing and inspection pass rates across project sizes
Notes: Permanent power eliminates temporary power cost. Switchgear lead time often critical path. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Electrical), Procurement (switchgear).

Description: Install sprinkler mains, branches, standpipe risers, and fire pump — main and branch line installation, sprinkler head rough-in, seismic bracing, FDC installation. Sprinkler head layout drives ceiling coordination. Gate: system tested and tagged. Duration: 3-5%.
Industry Standard: NFPA 13 (sprinklers); NFPA 14 (standpipes); NFPA 20 (fire pumps); NFPA 72 (fire alarm); IBC Chapter 9
Common Values: Fire protection rough-in complete; hydrostatic test reports; fire pump test; FDC installation
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs, observations); AutoSprink; Revit MEP; fire protection systems
Procore Coverage: High — inspections (hydrostatic test), daily logs, RFIs (head layout), observations all active
Benchmarking Use: Compare hydrostatic test pass rates and sprinkler head coordination conflict rates across building types
Notes: Head conflicts with lights, diffusers, and detectors are the most common coordination issue. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Fire Protection).

Description: Install raceways and cabling for data, security, fire alarm, and AV — conduit and cable tray, backbone and horizontal cabling, fire alarm wiring, security wiring. Often the last trade into the ceiling space. Gate: cabling complete and tested. Duration: 2-4%.
Industry Standard: TIA-568 (cabling); TIA-569 (pathways); NFPA 72 (fire alarm); BICSI (design and installation)
Common Values: Low voltage rough-in complete per area; cable test reports (certification); pathway inspection
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, RFIs); Fluke testing equipment; cable management systems; security integrator systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and RFIs track low voltage work; no dedicated cable certification tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare cable certification pass rates and data room readiness timelines across project types
Notes: Data room buildout is a coordination milestone. Cable testing and certification required for warranty. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Low Voltage & Technology).

Description: Frame interior partitions, soffits, and bulkheads — wall layout, metal stud framing, blocking for fixtures/casework, door frame setting, fire-rated assembly framing. Blocking for grab bars, casework, TV mounts must be coordinated with MEP. Gate: framing complete — ready for drywall. Duration: 3-5%.
Industry Standard: ASTM C645/C754 (steel framing); GA-600 (fire-rated assemblies); UL fire-rated assembly listings; IBC Ch. 7
Common Values: Framing complete per area; frame inspection (fire-rated walls); blocking location verification
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, RFIs, observations); Bluebeam; framing contractor layout tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections (fire-rated assemblies) and daily logs track framing; blocking coordination not structured
Benchmarking Use: Compare framing inspection pass rates and fire-rated wall deficiency rates across project types
Notes: Fire-rated wall framing inspection is a hold point. Missing blocking discovered during finish installation causes rework. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors).

Description: Hang and finish gypsum board — drywall hanging, taping and finishing, sanding, specialty boards (moisture/abuse/fire-rated), patching around MEP. Above-ceiling inspection must happen BEFORE drywall closes the ceiling. Gate: drywall complete — walls ready for finishes. Duration: 3-5%.
Industry Standard: GA-216 (gypsum application); GA-600 (fire-rated assemblies); ASTM C840; UL assemblies
Common Values: Drywall complete per area; fire-rated assembly inspection; Level 4/5 finish verification
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, observations); drywall contractor tracking; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — observations (quality — cracks, finish level), inspections (fire-rated), daily logs all track drywall
Benchmarking Use: Compare drywall quality deficiency rates and fire-rated assembly pass rates across project types
Notes: Drywall damage from other trades is a constant issue and major punch item source. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors), Quality Issues.

Description: Install suspension system for acoustic ceilings — main tee and cross tee, hanger wire, seismic bracing, grid leveling, access panel framing. Grid installation signals all above-ceiling MEP work must be complete. Gate: grid installed — above-ceiling work must be complete. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: ASTM C635/C636 (ceiling suspension); CISCA (ceiling standards); IBC (seismic bracing)
Common Values: Ceiling grid installed and level per area; above-ceiling MEP confirmed complete
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, coordination issues); ceiling contractor tools; laser leveling equipment
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and inspections track ceiling grid; coordination issues flag above-ceiling conflicts
Benchmarking Use: Compare above-ceiling inspection completion rates before grid installation across project types
Notes: Grid layout coordinates with light, sprinkler head, and diffuser locations. Last chance for above-ceiling inspection. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors, HVAC, Electrical, Fire Protection).

Description: Apply primers, paint, and specialty coatings — primer, first coat, second coat, touch-up, specialty coatings, wall protection. Paint is the most common punch item across ALL project types. Typically the first finish in a space. Gate: paint substantially complete. Duration: 3-5%.
Industry Standard: MPI (Master Painters Institute); SSPC (surface preparation); VOC regulations
Common Values: Paint complete per area; color verification; touch-up complete
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, punch items, observations); paint contractor tracking; color matching tools
Procore Coverage: High — punch items (touch-up is #1 category), observations (quality), daily logs all track paint
Benchmarking Use: Compare paint deficiency rates and touch-up punch item volume across project types
Notes: Other trades damaging paint is a constant problem requiring multiple touch-up rounds. Cross-domain: Quality Issues (paint/finish is #1 punch category universally).

Description: Install floor finishes — tile (ceramic, porcelain, stone), carpet (broadloom, tile), resilient (LVT, VCT, rubber), specialty (epoxy, terrazzo, polished concrete), base, transitions. Sequence: tile before casework, carpet last. Gate: flooring complete. Duration: 3-5%.
Industry Standard: TCNA Handbook (tile); CRI 104/105 (carpet); ASTM F710 (substrate prep); RFCI (resilient)
Common Values: Flooring complete per area; grout sealed; transitions installed; base complete
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, punch items, observations); flooring contractor systems; moisture testing equipment
Procore Coverage: High — punch items (cracks, lippage, seams), observations (quality), daily logs all track flooring
Benchmarking Use: Compare flooring deficiency rates by material type across project types
Notes: Floor protection critical after installation. Tile lippage and grout are common punch items. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors), Quality Issues.

Description: Install acoustic ceiling tile and specialty ceiling finishes — acoustic tile, specialty ceilings (wood, metal, drywall), ceiling access panels, edge trim. One of the last overhead activities. Gate: ceilings complete. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: ASTM E1264 (acoustical ceiling products); ASTM C423 (sound absorption); CISCA standards
Common Values: Ceilings complete per area; grid and tile aligned; access panels operational
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, punch items); ceiling contractor tools; laser leveling equipment
Procore Coverage: Medium — punch items (damaged tiles, alignment, sag) and daily logs track ceiling work
Benchmarking Use: Compare ceiling tile damage rates and replacement volume during punch list phase
Notes: Damaged tiles from other trades working above are a frequent punch item. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors).

Description: Install door slabs and finish hardware — door hanging, hinges, closers, locksets, pulls, keying, access control coordination, fire-rated door labeling. Top-3 punch item category on every project type. Gate: all doors hung and hardware operational. Duration: 2-3%.
Industry Standard: DHI (Door and Hardware Institute); BHMA (hardware standards); NFPA 80 (fire doors); ADA/ICC A117.1
Common Values: Doors and hardware complete; keying schedule implemented; fire door inspection
Source Systems: Procore (punch items, inspections, submittals); Allegion; ASSA ABLOY; hardware distributor systems
Procore Coverage: High — punch items (door adjustment, hardware alignment, closer force), inspections (fire doors) all active
Benchmarking Use: Compare door/hardware punch item rates and fire door inspection pass rates across project types
Notes: Hardware schedule is one of the most detailed submittals. Access control integration adds complexity. Cross-domain: Quality Issues (top-3 punch category).

Description: Install cabinets, countertops, trim, and built-in furnishings — cabinet installation, countertop templating and installation, trim and base molding, built-in shelving, toilet partitions and accessories. Gate: casework and accessories complete. Duration: 2-3%.
Industry Standard: AWI (Architectural Woodwork Institute); NACC (casework standards)
Common Values: Casework and millwork complete per area; countertops installed; accessories installed
Source Systems: Procore (punch items, daily logs, submittals); casework fabricator tracking; countertop templating systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — punch items (scratches, chips, alignment) and daily logs track casework; no dedicated module
Benchmarking Use: Compare casework deficiency rates and damage-during-installation rates across project types
Notes: Countertop templating happens after casework for exact dimensions. Toilet accessories typically one of the last installed items. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors).

Description: Install visible HVAC devices and controls — diffusers, grilles, thermostats, VAV box controls, damper actuators, BAS device installation, final duct connections. Must be complete before TAB. Gate: HVAC devices complete — ready for commissioning. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE (controls); BACnet/ASHRAE 135 (BAS protocol); SMACNA (ductwork connections)
Common Values: HVAC devices installed; controls wired and communicating; ready for TAB
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, punch items); BAS systems (Honeywell, Johnson Controls, Tridium)
Procore Coverage: Medium — punch items (diffuser alignment, thermostat location) and daily logs; commissioning via observations
Benchmarking Use: Compare HVAC device installation rates and controls commissioning timelines across building types
Notes: Controls programming often continues through commissioning. Thermostat/diffuser placement coordination with architect important. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC).

Description: Install visible plumbing fixtures — toilets, sinks, faucets, accessories, stop and waste valves, floor drain covers. Fixtures are damage-prone. Gate: plumbing fixtures complete and operational. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: International Plumbing Code; ADA/ICC A117.1 (accessible fixtures); WaterSense (EPA efficiency)
Common Values: Fixtures installed and operational; no-leak verification
Source Systems: Procore (punch items, daily logs, inspections); plumbing contractor systems
Procore Coverage: High — punch items (leaks, fixture alignment, trim finish) and daily logs track fixture installation
Benchmarking Use: Compare plumbing fixture punch item rates and leak detection rates across project types
Notes: High-volume punch item category. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Plumbing), Quality Issues.

Description: Install visible electrical devices and light fixtures — outlets, switches, cover plates, light fixtures, exit/emergency lighting, lighting controls, panel labeling. Major visual milestone. Gate: all devices and lighting operational. Duration: 2-3%.
Industry Standard: NEC/NFPA 70; IES (lighting design); ADA; emergency lighting codes
Common Values: Devices and lighting installed; circuits verified; lighting controls programmed
Source Systems: Procore (punch items, daily logs); lighting control systems (Lutron, Crestron)
Procore Coverage: High — punch items (cover plate alignment, fixture finish), daily logs, commissioning checklists all active
Benchmarking Use: Compare electrical device punch item rates and lighting control commissioning timelines
Notes: Cover plates and device alignment are high-volume punch items. Lighting control programming often continues into commissioning. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Electrical).

Description: Install visible fire alarm devices — smoke/heat detectors, pull stations, horn/strobes, duct detectors, device addressing, panel programming. Device placement must match approved fire alarm drawings. Gate: devices complete — ready for acceptance testing. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: NFPA 72 (fire alarm code); ADA (strobe candela and placement); IBC Chapter 9; UL 864 (panels)
Common Values: Fire alarm devices installed; system addressed and communicating; ready for acceptance test
Source Systems: Procore (inspections, punch items, daily logs); fire alarm contractor systems; programming tools
Procore Coverage: High — inspections (device placement), punch items (alignment, labeling), commissioning checklists all active
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire alarm device deficiency rates and AHJ acceptance test pass rates across building types
Notes: AHJ acceptance test is a required milestone. Strobe candela/placement per ADA is a common deficiency. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Fire Protection).

Description: Install visible low voltage devices — data jacks, cameras, card readers, intercoms, paging, AV equipment. Cable labeling and testing required. Gate: devices complete and tested. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: TIA-568 (cabling); TIA-606 (labeling); BICSI (installation); manufacturer certification requirements
Common Values: Devices installed; cable certified; systems communicating
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, punch items); Fluke testing equipment; security integrator systems; AV integrator systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — punch items (labeling, alignment) and daily logs; cable certification not structured in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Compare cable certification pass rates and integration testing timelines across project complexity
Notes: Integration testing between security, access control, and fire alarm is complex. Late owner changes to AV/data layout are common. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Low Voltage & Technology).

Category: --- COMMISSIONING & CLOSEOUT: systems testing, punch list, inspections, and turnover ---

Description: Verify each system component is properly installed before energizing or flowing — equipment installation verification, valve/damper tag verification, motor rotation checks, control device calibration, breaker verification. Commissioning agent (CxA) typically leads. Gate: all systems verified ready for startup. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0 (commissioning process); ASHRAE 202; LEED EAp1; WELL; IgCC
Common Values: Pre-functional checklists complete per system; deficiency lists; equipment readiness verification
Source Systems: Procore (observations, inspections); Cx Alloy; FacilityONE; CxPlanner; Excel commissioning templates
Procore Coverage: Medium — observations and inspections can track commissioning checklists; no dedicated CxA module
Benchmarking Use: Compare pre-functional deficiency rates and commissioning duration across building types
Notes: Many jurisdictions now require CxA. Catches installation errors before costly startup failures. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC, Plumbing, Electrical, Fire Protection, Elevators).

Description: Energize and start up individual systems for the first time — electrical energization sequence, boiler/chiller startup, pump/fan startup, fire pump acceptance test, elevator acceptance test, generator load bank test. Manufacturers require authorized rep for startup (warranty condition). Gate: all major systems operational. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: NETA (electrical testing); ASHRAE (HVAC startup); ASME A17.1 (elevators); NFPA 20 (fire pumps); NFPA 110 (generators)
Common Values: Startup reports per system; manufacturer startup certification; acceptance test reports
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections); manufacturer startup tools; NETA testing equipment; elevator test equipment
Procore Coverage: Low — inspections capture some startup events; no dedicated startup tracking or manufacturer certification module
Benchmarking Use: Compare startup deficiency rates and time-to-first-operation across building types and MEP complexity
Notes: Electrical energization must follow a specific safe sequence. Cross-domain: Building Elements (all MEP systems).

Description: Balance air and water systems to design flow rates — air system balancing (supply, return, exhaust), hydronic balancing (chilled, hot, condenser water), sound and vibration measurement. TAB contractor typically independent third party. Gate: TAB complete and report accepted. Duration: 1-3%.
Industry Standard: AABC (Associated Air Balance Council); NEBB (National Environmental Balancing Bureau); ASHRAE 111
Common Values: TAB report; air balance report; water balance report; deficiency list
Source Systems: TAB contractor instruments; Procore (observations, daily logs); HVAC BAS trending; air flow measurement tools
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated TAB tracking; observations and daily logs capture TAB activity informally
Benchmarking Use: Compare TAB deficiency rates and re-balancing frequency across building types
Notes: Results affect occupant comfort directly. Re-balancing after tenant buildout is common. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC).

Description: Verify systems work together correctly under various operating conditions — sequence of operations verification, failure mode testing, emergency power transfer, fire alarm integration, smoke control testing, elevator recall, BAS trending. Most complex commissioning phase. Gate: all integrated tests passed. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0; NFPA 72 (fire alarm integration); NFPA 92 (smoke control); IBC Chapter 9
Common Values: Integrated test reports; sequence verification reports; fire and life safety test reports; BAS trend logs
Source Systems: Procore (observations, inspections); BAS systems; fire alarm panels; smoke control systems; elevator controls
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated integrated testing module; observations can track test results informally
Benchmarking Use: Compare integrated test pass rates and fire/life safety test deficiency rates across building complexity
Notes: Tests systems interacting: fire alarm → elevator recall → smoke evacuation → emergency power. AHJ witnesses fire/life safety tests. Cross-domain: Building Elements (all systems).

Description: Confirm building performs to design intent under actual conditions — energy performance, thermal comfort, acoustics, lighting levels, indoor air quality, envelope performance. Some tests require seasonal conditions (heating vs. cooling season). Gate: performance verified and documented. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE 90.1 (energy); ASHRAE 55 (thermal comfort); LEED/WELL (certification); IgCC; IECC
Common Values: Performance verification report; energy model reconciliation; IEQ report; LEED/WELL documentation
Source Systems: Procore (observations); energy monitoring systems; IEQ test equipment; building envelope test equipment
Procore Coverage: None — no energy performance, IEQ, or building performance tracking modules
Benchmarking Use: Compare energy performance (actual vs. modeled) and LEED/WELL certification rates across project types
Notes: Required for LEED and WELL certification. Envelope commissioning increasingly required by code. Cross-domain: Project Attributes (sustainability rating).

Description: Identify and correct remaining deficiencies before owner acceptance — architect punchlist walk, owner punchlist walk, deficiency documentation, trade-by-trade correction, re-inspection, sign-off. Typical project: 500-5,000 punch items. Paint, doors/hardware, and MEP devices consistently top categories. Gate: punch list substantially complete. Duration: 2-4%.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (substantial completion); AIA G704 (certificate of substantial completion)
Common Values: Punch list generated; punch items corrected; architect sign-off; owner acceptance
Source Systems: Procore (punch items); PlanGrid; Fieldwire; BIM 360 Field; Bluebeam; Raken
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated punch_item module with workflow_status, type, trade tracking; widely adopted
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch item volume per unit area and deficiency rates by trade across project types
Notes: Most labor-intensive closeout activity. Multiple walks are normal. Cross-domain: Quality Issues (insight #4 — punch items are strongest quality signal).

Description: Secure regulatory sign-offs required for occupancy — fire marshal inspection, building department final, elevator inspection, health department, certificate of occupancy (CO), temporary CO (TCO). Gate: certificate of occupancy issued. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: IBC/ICC (building code); NFPA (fire code); ASME A17.1 (elevators); ADA; state/local health codes
Common Values: Certificate of occupancy (CO); elevator certificate; fire marshal sign-off; final inspection reports
Source Systems: Procore (inspections); municipal inspection portals; fire marshal scheduling systems
Procore Coverage: High — inspections module tracks AHJ inspections and hold points; inspection_items with status tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare CO milestone achievement rates and inspection failure/re-inspection rates across jurisdictions
Notes: TCO is common when portions of work remain. Each AHJ has unique requirements. Cross-domain: Project Attributes (location drives requirements).

Description: Compile record documents reflecting actual constructed conditions — as-built drawing markup, O&M manual compilation, warranty compilation, spare parts and attic stock delivery, equipment data sheets. Contractually required — final payment often withheld until complete. Gate: record documents delivered and accepted. Duration: 1-2%.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (record documents); CSI (O&M manual organization); ASHRAE Guideline 4 (O&M documentation)
Common Values: As-built drawings; O&M manuals; warranty binder; spare parts inventory; equipment schedules; keying records
Source Systems: Procore (document management, drawings); BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; SharePoint; Box
Procore Coverage: Medium — document management and drawing modules exist; as-built markups possible but not structured
Benchmarking Use: Compare as-built documentation completion rates and delivery timelines across project types
Notes: Quality varies enormously — digital O&M delivery increasingly expected. Cross-domain: Building Elements (all systems documented).

Description: Train owner's facilities staff on building systems operation and maintenance — HVAC operations, BAS, fire alarm, elevator, security, electrical distribution, plumbing. Often rushed or skipped — leads to operational problems. Gate: training complete and signed off. Duration: <1%.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0 (training requirements); LEED (enhanced commissioning training credit)
Common Values: Training sign-off sheets; training recordings; supplemental documentation
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, action plans); training recording tools; manufacturer training programs
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated training tracking module; daily logs or action plans used informally
Benchmarking Use: Compare training completion rates and post-occupancy warranty claim correlation across building types
Notes: Quality of training directly affects warranty claim volume. Manufacturer reps typically conduct training. Cross-domain: Warranty & Operations.

Description: Complete commercial and legal close of all contracts — final change order reconciliation, final pay application, retainage release, lien waiver collection, consent of surety, final accounting, contract close letter. Gate: all contracts formally closed. Duration: 1-3%.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (final payment, retainage); state lien law; UCC (Uniform Commercial Code); surety bond requirements
Common Values: Final pay application approved; retainage released; all lien waivers collected; contract formally closed
Source Systems: Procore (commitments, pay applications, change orders); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Textura; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — commitments, pay applications, and change order modules track financial closeout; lien waiver tracking available
Benchmarking Use: Compare retainage release timelines and disputed change order rates across project types
Notes: Retainage (typically 5-10% of contract value) is a major financial event. Lien waiver collection from all tiers can be complex. Cross-domain: Financial (all cost surfaces reconciled).

Category: --- POST-CONSTRUCTION: warranty period and facility transition to operations ---

Description: Contractor responsible for correcting defects discovered after substantial completion — warranty claim response, defect investigation, repair coordination, manufacturer warranty coordination, 11-month warranty walk. General warranty: 1 year; roofing: 10-20 years (manufacturer); structural: varies by jurisdiction. Gate: warranty expired.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (warranty provisions); manufacturer warranties; state statute of repose; UCC warranty
Common Values: Warranty claims resolved; 11-month warranty walk report; warranty expiration documentation
Source Systems: Procore (observations, punch items); CMMS (Maximo, ServiceNow); facility work order systems; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated warranty module; observations and punch items can track defects but not warranty lifecycle
Benchmarking Use: Compare warranty claim rates and 11-month walk deficiency volume across building types
Notes: 11-month walk catches items before warranty expires. Cross-domain: Quality Issues (warranty claims correlate with construction deficiency rates).

Description: Verify HVAC performance across heating and cooling seasons not available during initial commissioning — heating season verification, cooling season verification, BAS setpoint optimization, energy monitoring review. Gate: all seasonal testing complete (typically 12 months post-occupancy).
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0 (seasonal testing); LEED EAp1 (enhanced commissioning)
Common Values: Seasonal commissioning reports; optimized setpoint documentation; energy performance confirmation
Source Systems: BAS systems (Honeywell, Johnson Controls); energy monitoring platforms; Procore (observations — rare)
Procore Coverage: None — no commissioning or energy performance module; Procore not typically used post-construction
Benchmarking Use: Compare seasonal energy performance variance and setpoint optimization outcomes across climate zones
Notes: First full year reveals actual performance across all conditions. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC).

Description: Transfer operational knowledge and responsibility from construction team to facilities team — maintenance schedule establishment, spare parts inventory, vendor contact transfer, BAS access transfer, service contract establishment. Gate: facility fully transitioned to operations.
Industry Standard: IFMA (facility management); ASHRAE Guideline 4 (O&M); BOMA (building operations)
Common Values: Maintenance plan; vendor contact list; service contracts; BAS operational handoff; facility condition baseline
Source Systems: CMMS (Maximo, ServiceNow, Archibus); BAS systems; Procore (document handoff only); FMX; UpKeep
Procore Coverage: None — Procore is construction-phase; facility management requires dedicated CMMS/IWMS systems
Benchmarking Use: Compare facility transition completeness and first-year maintenance cost across building types
Notes: Quality of this transition determines decades of building maintenance. Poor transitions lead to deferred maintenance and premature failure. Cross-domain: all domains.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from phase tracking entities ---

Description: Actual phase duration compared to baseline schedule — (actual days - planned days) / planned days × 100. Positive = behind schedule. Measured per phase and sub-phase using schedule milestones and activity completion data.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (schedule management); AACE RP 52R-06 (schedule assessment)
Common Values: Expressed as percentage or days; typical range -10% to +50%; median commercial project ~+12%
Source Systems: Oracle P6; MS Project; Procore (schedule_task, project_dates); Asta Powerproject
Procore Coverage: Medium — schedule_task has durations; schedule_task_changes tracks deltas; project_dates has milestones
Benchmarking Use: Compare phase slippage across project types and value bands to identify systematic schedule risk
Notes: Most slippage occurs in procurement (long-lead items), enclosure (weather), and commissioning (integration complexity).

Description: Earned value metric: SPI = Earned Value / Planned Value. SPI < 1.0 = behind schedule, SPI > 1.0 = ahead. Calculated at project or phase level using weighted activity completion against the baseline schedule.
Industry Standard: AACE RP 10S-90 (earned value); PMI PMBOK (EVM); ANSI/EIA-748 (EVMS)
Common Values: SPI typically 0.7-1.2; healthy range 0.9-1.1; government projects often require formal EVM
Source Systems: Oracle P6 (native EVM); MS Project (EVM); Deltek; Procore (no native EVM)
Procore Coverage: Low — schedule exists but no earned value calculation; would require weighted activity completion data
Benchmarking Use: Compare SPI trends across phases to identify where schedule recovery or acceleration occurs
Notes: Government/institutional projects most likely to use formal EVM. Commercial projects rely on milestone tracking instead.

Description: Percentage of phase transition milestones achieved on or before scheduled date — on-time milestones / total milestones × 100. Tracks critical path gates between phases.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (milestone management); AACE (schedule control)
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; typical range 40-80%; top-quartile projects >75%
Source Systems: Oracle P6; MS Project; Procore (project_dates, schedule_task milestones); Asta Powerproject
Procore Coverage: Medium — project_dates tracks key milestones; schedule_calendar_item has calendar entries
Benchmarking Use: Compare milestone hit rates across project types and identify which transitions are most frequently delayed
Notes: Dry-in, permanent power, and CO are the most commonly tracked milestones. Late milestones cascade downstream.

Description: Percentage of a phase's duration that overlaps with adjacent phases — overlap days / total phase days × 100. Higher overlap = more concurrent/fast-track execution. Normal for procurement to overlap with construction.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (fast tracking); AACE (schedule compression)
Common Values: Ranges 0% (sequential) to 80%+ (highly fast-tracked); typical 15-40%
Source Systems: Oracle P6; MS Project; Procore (schedule_task); Asta Powerproject
Procore Coverage: Low — schedule tasks exist but phase-level overlap requires classification of tasks into phases
Benchmarking Use: Compare fast-tracking rates across delivery methods (design-build typically higher than design-bid-build)
Notes: Fast-tracking increases rework risk when upstream phases change. Cross-domain: Rework Cost (insight #1 — design changes during construction).

Description: RFIs generated during each phase, normalized by duration (RFIs per week) or project value (RFIs per $M). Indicates information gap intensity at each project stage.
Industry Standard: CSI (RFI process); AIA A201 (contractor RFI rights)
Common Values: Typical total: 200-2,000 RFIs per project; design phase: 5-15/week; peak construction: 10-30/week
Source Systems: Procore (rfis); PlanGrid; BIM 360; Submittal Exchange; Newforma
Procore Coverage: High — rfis table with timestamps, status, schedule_impact, predicted_topic (ML-classified); 17,540+ cos
Benchmarking Use: Compare RFI volume curves across project types to identify information-heavy phases and reduce bottlenecks
Notes: RFI volume peaks during structure and MEP rough-in. predicted_topic enables classification without NLP. Cross-domain: Design Change Impact (insight #31).

Description: Punch items per unit area (per 1,000 SF) or per trade during closeout — calculated from punch item creation dates and project gross area. Indicates construction quality and closeout efficiency.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (substantial completion criteria); AIA G704
Common Values: Typical: 0.5-5.0 items per 1,000 SF; top-3 categories: paint, doors/hardware, MEP devices
Source Systems: Procore (punch_item); PlanGrid; Fieldwire; BIM 360 Field
Procore Coverage: High — punch_item table with workflow_status, type_name, creation dates; 22.3M items, 5,470+ cos
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch item density across project types to identify which trades drive the most closeout work
Notes: Cross-domain: Quality Issues (insight #4 — 18 keyword categories), Rework Cost (insight #1 — punch items are trailing indicator).

Description: Average days from submittal submission to final approval — calculated per submittal, then aggregated by trade, spec section, or phase. Longer cycles delay procurement and fabrication start.
Industry Standard: CSI (submittal management); AIA A201 (review periods)
Common Values: Typical: 14-30 days per cycle; resubmittals add 7-21 days; critical path (steel, curtain wall): 21-45 days
Source Systems: Procore (submittals); Submittal Exchange; Newforma; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: High — submittals table with created_at, due_date, overdue flag, status; 8.9M+ records
Benchmarking Use: Compare submittal cycle times by spec section to identify review bottlenecks across project types
Notes: 718K overdue submittals in 2-year window. Overdue submittals correlate with material delays. Cross-domain: Procurement (Submittals sub-phase).
